insights: Development department processes expenses 8.5x faster than the next fastest department (IT at 8.46 days), with a median processing time of just 1.0 day—tightly concentrated between 0–2 days.

The speed advantage is not attributable to category mix or expense amount patterns, as Development shows only minor differences (≤8.0 percentage points in category distribution; 7% lower median expense amount) compared to other departments.

Development’s processing speed is fully explained by two exclusive users—Ramon Amaral and Cyril Behen—with zero user overlap across departments, indicating the advantage is person-specific rather than practice- or process-based.

Development exhibits a distinct state distribution (69% Processed, 12% Pending, 10% Declined, 9% Submitted) versus all other departments (86% Processed, 14% Declined, 0% Pending/Submitted), suggesting fundamentally different workflow execution—not just faster completion but active handling of non-final states.

Processed_date missingness is lowest in Development (14.3%) versus HR (55.6%), reinforcing that Development’s data reflects more complete and timely system updates—consistent with real-time operational discipline rather than systemic reporting lag.

No other department shows meaningful differentiation in processing time (all cluster tightly at 8–14 days median), confirming that Development’s performance is an outlier—not part of a broader departmental efficiency gradient.
summary: The goal was to identify transferable practices driving faster expense processing in the Development department; however, modeling reveals its 8.5x speed advantage stems exclusively from two dedicated users with no cross-department presence—not from replicable process design, category focus, or expense profile. Neither category distribution nor expense amounts meaningfully distinguish Development, and its unique state distribution (active handling of Pending/Submitted cases) further signals individual-driven execution rather than standardized workflow improvements. The absence of any comparable performance among other departments confirms this is not a scalable department-level pattern. To improve enterprise-wide efficiency, attention should shift from emulating Development’s ‘practices’ to diagnosing why those two users operate so effectively—and whether their capacity, authority, tooling, or role scope can be intentionally replicated or supported elsewhere.
